data.title: Majority of Americans say Trump's rhetoric about China hurts Chinese-Americans: Survey
data.description:
A majority of Americans think that President Trump’s rhetoric and language when discussing China and relations with Beijing “negatively impacts” how people of Chinese descent are treated in the U.S., according to a new survey. 

Fifty-four percent of respondents said Trump’s rhetoric has a negative impact on people of Chinese descent, 41 percent reported neither a positive or negative impact and just five percent reporting a positive impact, the survey, a collaboration between the Committee of 100 and the National Opinion Research Center at the University of Chicago, found.

“U.S.-China relations deeply affect how Chinese Americans here are perceived,” said Sheryl Wudunn, a member of Committee of 100 and a Chinese-American reporter, said during a webinar last week. “We all saw what happened during COVID when COVID was referred to as Kung flu or China virus and what that did to Chinese Americans.” 

Trump said he backed off that rhetoric at the request of Chinese President Xi Jinping, during remarks to the World Economic Forum in Davos last week. 

“I used to call it the China virus, but he said, do you think you could use a different name and I decided to do that because why should we have a problem over that?” Trump said.

White House Deputy Press Secretary Anna Kelly, responding to the survey, touted the president’s China policies.

“What comments?” she said in an emailed statement to The Hill. “President Trump has maintained a positive relationship with President Xi, while simultaneously putting America First by protecting our national security, leveling the playing field for our farmers, and securing an agreement from China to crack down on fentanyl precursors.”

The percentage of Americans who have an unfavorable opinion of China has climbed significantly in the last 20 years, according to Pew Research Center. Despite perceptions improving slightly in the past two years, about eight in ten Americans still report an unfavorable view of China. 

Rhetoric from political leaders is among the factors driving these attitudes, the survey found. The language used in news coverage also made a difference. 

Researchers found that participants who read a fake news article that assigned blame for intellectual property theft to the “Chinese government” were more likely to view Chinese immigrants “very favorably” compared to those who read an article attributing the actions to “China.” They were also more likely to see Chinese Americans as “very loyal” to the U.S.

While the results are particular to China and people of Chinese descent, Research lead Sam Collitt said differentiating people versus government in language could impact the perceptions of those descended from other foreign adversaries like North Korea and Russia as well. 

“We saw that that kind of precision in language really does have a bearing on the kind of downstream effects that has on perceptions of people of Chinese descent,” Collitt said. “And given the fact that ‘China’ is what’s used in most news reporting and most politicians, messaging, policy matters, is not ‘Chinese government,’ it’s ‘China.’” 

The survey, released on Jan. 21, also found that such language may be impacting views of whether Chinese-Americans were more loyal to China than to the U.S. After reading an article using “Chinese government,” 21 percent of respondents said Chinese-Americans were “much more loyal to the U.S.,” compared to 11 percent when the article used “China.” 

These attitudes and language choices have a significant impact on Chinese-Americans, said ,” said Cindy Tsai, executive vice president and general counsel of Committee of 100.

“I do think that there is that direct impact between the belonging and the mental health and feeling secure in this country,” she said. 

Sixty percent of Americans also reported wanting the U.S. to work more closely with China on diplomatic issues and policies impacting both countries. This included 65 percent of Democrats, 63 percent of Republicans and 39 percent of independents. 

Collitt said the majorities in both parties indicate “pretty clear bipartisan support for a better working relationship,” during a webinar last week. 

“The fact that we find that people are making a direct connection between what relations look like between two countries, as well as specifically how that’s messaged, from party leaders, from the government, from media, and how that has a direct bearing on people’s lives, and so far as how they’re treated, as their sense of belonging, mental health, I think the fact that that through line is being drawn by the American public, at least indicated by our data, is pretty astounding,” Collitt said. “And I think it speaks to the kind of the severity of the effects that are taking place.”

The survey also asked respondents whether they supported a ban on students and scholars from China coming to the U.S. to collaborate on research after the Trump administration threatened to “aggressively” revoke visas for Chinese graduate students affiliated with the Chinese Communist Party or studying in “critical fields.” 

Multiple Republican lawmakers have also proposed blocking federal funding to researchers with any ties to China citing concerns that the country could be exploiting open research for military gains.

Despite the administration citing national security threats, only 23 percent of Americans support prohibiting Chinese students and researchers from coming to the U.S. to do research, according to the survey. This includes just 16 percent of Democrats, 34 percent of Republicans and 18 percent of independents. 

“As we try to make sure the United States remains the leader in terms of scientific research we need to make sure we’re attracting the people who are the leading scientific minds in the world and these will include Chinese,” Madelina Hsu, a professor of history at the University of Maryland-College Park, said as a panelist during the webinar. 

“To try to label on the basis of race or national origin who may or may not be spies is a crude instrument. It’s not going to improve our national security.”

data.title: Lockheed Martin to quadruple production of THAAD missile interceptors for Pentagon
data.description:
Defense contractor Lockheed Martin announced Thursday it will quadruple its production of Terminal High Altitude Area Defense (THAAD) interceptors, which are used to protect U.S. military bases and allies, over seven years under a new agreement with the Pentagon. 

Lockheed Martin said it will ramp up production of THAAD interceptors, the antimissile batteries dispatched to the Middle East, from 96 to 400 per year. 

The system, which can intercept missiles inside or outside the atmosphere, is interoperable with other ballistic missile defense systems. 

“We are committed to further building on the Department of War’s vision for advancing acquisition reform with additional framework agreements for the critical munitions needed by the U.S. military and our allies,” Lockheed Martin Chair and CEO Jim Taiclet said in a statement. 

“Today’s agreement to quadruple THAAD production means we will have more interceptors available than ever before to deter our adversaries,” Taiclet added. 

Each THAAD interceptor costs $12.77 million in fiscal year 2025, according to the Pentagon’s documents.

Each THAAD battery consists of five major components: launchers, interceptors, a fire control unit, a radar and support equipment. 

The U.S. military utilized THAAD batteries during the conflict between Israel and Iran last June, depleting stockpiles and raising concerns about the shortages of both the intercepts and systems.

data.title: Senate Democrats demand testimony from Noem after Minneapolis shootings
data.description:
Senate Homeland Security Committee Democrats are demanding Department of Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem appear before the panel amid scrutiny over the killing of two Minneapolis residents by immigration officers.

“You have presided over an increasingly dangerous and untenable breakdown in trust in federal law enforcement across the country, and your actions in the wake of these tragedies have weakened the Department’s credibility, enflamed tensions, harmed public safety, and divided Americans,” Sen. Gary Peters (Mich.), the top Democrat on the Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee, wrote with the other Democratic members. 

“You have authorized actions that have resulted in a vast overreach of the Department’s authorities and have cost innocent lives. Masked federal agents from DHS have violently assaulted peaceful protestors, arrested American citizens without cause, undermined protections under the Fourth Amendment, and shot and killed two Americans — Renee Good and Alex Pretti — who, contrary to your own baseless assertions, clearly did not pose a lethal threat.” 

Noem’s office did not immediately respond to request for comment.

Noem and U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) leaders have been under growing pressure after the deaths of Good and Pretti, including for their claims defending the personnel who shot and killed them.

Even before the two shootings, Noem faced grumbling from Democrats who have said they have had difficulty getting her to commit to appearances before Congress.

She left a House Homeland Security Committee hearing titled “Worldwide Threats to the Homeland” early for a meeting that was later canceled and then did not appear for the same hearing when it was convened on the Senate side.

She’s previously faced calls by Democrats on the House committee to appear for testimony there.

“The fact that you have doubled down to justify these indefensible killings in the face of widespread public outrage has caused DHS to lose credibility and calls into question your ability to lead this Department and its tens of thousands of federal agents entrusted to ensure public safety and to protect the homeland,” the group wrote.

“As the Secretary of Homeland Security, you have an obligation to appear before the Senate Committee with jurisdiction over DHS and the security of our homeland, as the urgency of this moment requires.”

data.title: Tillis rails against Noem on Senate floor for North Carolina hurricane response
data.description:
Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) slammed Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Secretary Kristi Noem for delays in providing disaster recovery aid to his state on the Senate floor Thursday.

“The data clearly shows that something is seriously wrong here,” Tillis said, pointing to a chart showing the federal government’s responses to hurricanes Helene, Matthew and Florence. “Under Secretary Noem’s lack of leadership, FEMA has invented an entirely new set of bureaucracies, the likes of which I’ve never seen. And I’m in a state that deals with a disaster almost every season.”

In the fall of 2024, western North Carolina was devastated by Helene, with 108 verified storm-related deaths in the state, according to its department of health and human services.

As of earlier this month, the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA), housed within DHS, says it has provided over $1 billion in aid to the state. But The New York Times reported Tuesday that roughly $17 billion in disaster funding for states is subject to extra review by Noem, causing delays.

The delay applies to funds that have already been approved by regional FEMA offices, the outlet noted. 

Tillis said the reporting “sadly confirms what the people of western North Carolina have known for the past year.”

“Now under Noem’s leadership, which is supposed to be more efficient, more effective and more responsive to the disaster in western North Carolina and the other states that were affected look like the EKG of someone who’s having a heart attack,” he said, pointing to the chart, which shows that the federal government’s response to Helene featured significantly more delays than its response to Matthew and Florence, storms that impacted North Carolina in 2016 and 2018, respectively.

Tillis also said that Noem is running FEMA “like some amateur.” The Hill has reached out to DHS and FEMA for comment.

Tillis has criticized Noem throughout this week for her handling of the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti by Border Patrol agents in Minneapolis. Hours after the shooting, Noem said without evidence that Pretti “committed an act of domestic terrorism” before being shot. 

“What she’s done in Minnesota should be disqualifying. She should be out of a job,” Tillis told reporters Tuesday “It’s just amateur-ish. It’s terrible. It’s making the president look bad on policy that he won on. [President Trump] won on a strong message on immigration. Now, nobody’s talking about that. … They’re talking about the incompetence of the leader of the Homeland Security [Department].”

data.title: House Democrat: 'Extremely unusual' Gabbard would be present at Fulton County operation
data.description:
Rep. Joaquin Castro (D-Texas) called Director of National Intelligence (DNI) Tulsi Gabbard’s involvement in a recent FBI search in Fulton County, Ga., “extremely unusual.”

Gabbard was captured on camera by Reuters photographer Elijah Nouvelage talking on the phone next to a vehicle filled with packed boxes. 

Castro, a member of the House Permanent Select Committee on Intelligence, called for a congressional investigation into the incident during an interview with CNN on Thursday. 

“It’s extremely unusual that the DNI would be present for this type of domestic operation, and it’s something that the intelligence committee should investigate right away, should exercise its oversight power and bring Tulsi Gabbard into the committee to explain exactly what happened,” Castro said. 

The FBI was investigating 2020 voting records at the Fulton County elections office. The county is the center of President Trump’s claims of election fraud, which resulted in the Fulton County District Attorney Fani Willis investigating and indicting the president and 18 others on charges tied to attempts to overturn the 2020 election. 

After Willis was disqualified from the case due to her relationship with a top prosecutor, the case was dropped in 2025. 

“Donald Trump has been fixated with this idea that votes were stolen from him in 2020 in the state of Georgia, and he won’t let that go,” Castro said. “And now he’s sent out the FBI and other agents in his government to try to prove something that he imagined happened six years ago.”

An official told NewsNation that Gabbard “has a pivotal role in election security and protecting the integrity of our elections against interference, including operations targeting voting systems, databases, and election infrastructure. She has and will continue to take action on President Trump’s directive to secure our elections and work with our interagency partners to do so.”

Castro is the latest Democrat to express concerns over the FBI search. 

Sen. Mark Warner (D-Va.) shared the Reuters image of Gabbard in a post on the social platform X on Wednesday with the caption: “Why is Tulsi Gabbard at an FBI raid on an election office in Fulton County?”

Warner, the vice chairman of the Senate Select Committee on Intelligence, called Gabbard’s presence at the search “a serious breach of trust” in a follow-up post, noting the director was required to notify Congress of “relevant national security concerns.”

data.title: Shutdown talks hinge on length of Homeland Security funding stopgap
data.description:
Senators say that negotiations over a stalled government funding package are hung up over the question of how long to temporarily fund the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) at current levels to give the White House and Senate Democrats more time to negotiate immigration enforcement reforms.

Republican senators said Thursday that the White House is on board with Democrats’ request to strip a Homeland Security appropriations bill out of a six-bill government funding package that needs to pass by 11:59 p.m. Friday to avoid a government shutdown.

But Republican and Democratic negotiators can’t agree on the length of a continuing resolution (CR) to temporarily fund the Homeland Security Department while the White House and Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer (N.Y.) hammer out reforms to Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE).

“We’ll see kind of where the discussions are going with them and the White House on that,” Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) said of the length of the stopgap to fund Homeland Security separately from other federal departments.

Democrats want a two-week funding stopgap for Homeland Security to put pressure on Republicans to agree quickly to reforms. They want a Feb. 13 funding deadline for the department.

But Republicans are saying that Homeland Security needs to be funded for longer than that, anticipating that it will take weeks to find common ground on the reforms Schumer and Senate Democrats demanded this week.

“My understanding is the White House wants longer. I don’t personally think two weeks is enough but I’m not sure any amount of time is enough in this environment. I hope I’m wrong but I think DHS is going to be shut down for a while,” Sen. John Kennedy (R-La.) said.

Thune told reporters that a two-week funding stopgap is probably too short to work out the differences over proposed reforms to Trump’s immigration enforcement operations.

“The two-week CR probably means there’s going to be another two-week CR and maybe another two-week CR after that,” Thune said.

He noted that it takes about a week to process a spending bill through the Senate, which means a deal to fund Homeland Security through fiscal year 2026 would have to be reached within days just to give the House and Senate enough time to process it.

Thune and other Republicans say they hope a deal could be worked by Thursday night or Friday night.

Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) said Democrats and Republicans are discussing a stopgap that would last between two and six weeks.

“Two weeks is probably too short. Probably four or five weeks is a happy middle ground,” he said.

Six of the 12 annual appropriations measures have already been enacted into law and Senate leaders are now negotiating a deal to pass five of the remaining six spending bills through the upper chamber this week, leaving aside Homeland Security appropriations.

“I think most people realize we need to take the win. My goodness, how many times do we get an opportunity to pass five regular-order appropriations bills? That’s about as rare as unicorns around here,” Tillis said.

“Take the win there and then negotiate … long-term funding for DHS,” he said.

data.title: Jeffries demands ban on deportation of US citizens as part of DHS reforms
data.description:
House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) on Thursday said House Democrats will demand a ban on the deportation of American citizens as a condition of winning their support for legislation funding the Department of Homeland Security (DHS).

“In what country are we living in if it’s controversial to prohibit [the deportation] of American citizens?” he said during a press briefing in the Capitol. “That shouldn’t even be a discussion.”

The DHS funding bill had already passed the House this month and was on its way to Senate approval, but it’s hit a wall in the wake of last Saturday’s fatal shooting of Alex Pretti, an ICU nurse, by federal immigration agents in Minneapolis. 

The killing has infuriated Democrats, who voted Thursday in the Senate to block a six-bill appropriations “minibus” to protest the inclusion of the DHS portion. The critics are vowing to continue that opposition until Republicans agree to new language in the DHS bill establishing guardrails on the conduct of the federal officers under the department. 

Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) on Wednesday laid out the changes he and Senate Democrats are demanding. They include new warrant requirements prior to searches and arrests; the establishment of a blanket code of conduct applying to all federal law enforcement personnel; a ban on agents wearing face masks; and new body camera mandates. 

“This is not border security, this is not law and order, this is chaos — created at the top and felt in so many of our neighborhoods,” Schumer said in announcing the list.

Jeffries broadly endorsed each of those reforms, though he cautioned that House Democrats will evaluate any legislative changes that come over from the Senate before endorsing any reform bills.

Jeffries’s proposed ban on the deportation of U.S. citizens goes a step further than Schumer did. He said it’s necessary given numerous reports indicating that American citizens have been wrongly deported as part of Trump’s aggressive deportation operations. DHS officials have denied that the agency is targeting or has deported U.S. citizens.

“In what country are we living in where ICE and DHS have free rein to detain and deport American citizens? That’s inconsistent with the Constitution,” he said. “And so if the extremists don’t get it, it should be made explicit.”

Jeffries also stipulated that there must be language in any new DHS bill empowering state and local law enforcers to conduct their own investigations, and perhaps bring their own charges in cases of alleged misconduct by DHS officers. 

“Those investigations cannot be conducted independently by the Department of Justice or by anyone closely associated with the Trump administration. They have no credibility to investigate anything,” Jeffries said. “And so, state and local law enforcement should explicitly have the authority to investigate crimes that are committed by DHS agents in their state and local jurisdictions.”

data.title: Watch live: Trump to unveil federal initiative addressing drug addiction
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday will sign an executive order that aims to coordinate a federal plan to combat drug addiction and substance abuse.

The “White House Great American Recovery Initiative” will advise federal agencies to issue grants in support of addiction recovery and education, according to the fact sheet obtained by CBS News. The panel will advise the federal government on how to integrate programs to aid with drug abuse prevention, intervention, treatment, recovery and support.

The order comes as the administration has ramped up its fight against drug trafficking, including deadly strikes on alleged narcotic-smuggling boats in the Caribbean.

The announcement from the Oval Office is scheduled to begin at 4 p.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Senate panel advances key crypto bill without Democratic support
data.description:
The Senate Agriculture Committee advanced part of a key cryptocurrency bill Thursday without Democratic support.

The panel voted 12-11 along party lines to advance its portion of the market structure legislation, which aims to clearly divide oversight of crypto markets between two financial regulators.

The vote marks a major step forward for the bill in the Senate, where Republicans and crypto-friendly Democrats have been negotiating for more than six months. But the lack of bipartisan support underscores the long and likely bumpy ahead for the legislation.

“While the conversations were cordial and substantive, what we found is that in some issues, there were fundamental policy disagreements,” Senate Agriculture Chair John Boozman (R-Ark.) said at Thursday’s markup.

“In a democracy, that’s okay,” he continued. “After months of work, we have made significant progress, really significant progress, working together. Now it’s time to move this process forward.”

Boozman and Sen. Cory Booker (D-N.J.), the top Democrat on the subcommittee that oversees digital assets, initially released a bipartisan market structure draft in November, although the text left several key issues unresolved.

This distinguished the panel from the Senate Banking Committee, where Republicans released a draft last July and have yet to secure support from crypto-friendly Democrats.

The Senate Agriculture Committee shares jurisdiction over the crypto bill with the Senate Banking Committee, as they respectively oversee the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) and Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) — the two regulators at issue in the bill.

Despite early bipartisan agreement, Boozman released a new draft ahead of Thursday’s markup that lacked Democratic support.

“I was excited,” Booker said Thursday, adding, “But then I come back to Washington and start the year with the news that my Republican colleagues were walking away from the bipartisan process.”

“To be clear, the product before us today is not the bipartisan draft that we were working on,” he continued. “It was negotiated with two parties at the table, but it isn’t in accord with the principles we laid out in November in a bipartisan way. However, despite this, I still deeply believe in the work that we’ve done.”

Democrats have continued to voice concerns with how the legislation handles ethics and national security issues, as well as the makeup of the CFTC.

Ethics has been a core sticking point, particularly as President Trump and his family have increasingly gotten involved in the crypto business, with ventures like World Liberty Financial and the $TRUMP memecoin.

“This is ridiculous that the president of the United States and his family have made billions of dollars off of this industry and are still trying to create a framework here without the kind of ethics that would prevent this kind of gross corruption in our country that would undermine our nation’s democracy,” Booker said.

Democrats have pushed for language in the market structure bill that would bar elected officials and their families from issuing or endorsing digital assets. Sen. Michael Bennet (D-Colo.) put forward an amendment Thursday proposing such a provision.

The amendment was ultimately voted down, with Boozman suggesting the issue falls outside the jurisdiction of the Senate Agriculture Committee.

The makeup of the CFTC has also been a point of concern. The agency, which is typically headed up by a five-person bipartisan commission, currently has only one commissioner — CFTC chair Michael Selig. Selig was nominated by Trump and confirmed by the Senate last month.

This comes as the relatively low-profile agency is poised to take on significant new responsibilities overseeing crypto markets. Democrats have argued for a provision requiring the CFTC have a minimum number of members from both parties to move forward with rulemaking.

Despite the partisan nature of Thursday’s markup, several Democrats underscored that they are hoping to continue bipartisan negotiations.

“I expect the vote today in this committee will probably fall on party lines,” Sen. Adam Schiff (D-Calif.) said. “That is not reflective, though, I think of the progress that we’ve made.”

Boozman told reporters after the vote that senators would be continuing talks Thursday afternoon.

“I think we reached a little bit of an impasse, and so we felt like we needed to move it forward, and this just gives us the opportunity to have something that has passed and be able to change it as we need to,” he added.

The Senate Agriculture Committee vote comes after a markup in the Senate Banking Committee was postponed earlier this month in the face of industry concerns.

Senate Banking Chair Tim Scott (R-S.C.) made the announcement the night before the markup was set to go forward, after Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong said he would not be able to support the latest version of the bill.

The panel had released a new draft of its portion of the legislation ahead of the markup. A provision on stablecoin yield, in particular, faced pushback from the crypto industry, which has lobbied against such language for months.

data.title: Schumer lays out immigration demands to avoid government shutdown
data.description:
Democrats have been getting hammered with the same question for months: Where’s your plan?  

Well, now they’re putting it on paper. And it’s really not some abstract, academic wish list; it’s three very specific demands tied to Homeland Security funding, coming as the clock ticks toward a shutdown deadline and after federal agents shot and killed Alex Pretti in Minneapolis last weekend.  

Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer says his caucus is united around three main objectives, and when you listen to what they are, the bigger point is simple: immigration enforcement can exist, but it can’t operate with no rules, no transparency and no accountability.  

  
“First we need to end roving patrols,” the New York Democrat said. “We want to tighten the rules governing the use of warrants and require ICE coordination with state and local law enforcement.” 

“Second, we want to enforce accountability; there needs to be a uniform code of conduct and accountability. Federal agents should be held to the same use of force policies that apply to state and local law enforcement and be held accountable when they violate these rules. We need independent investigations to help enforce accountability.”  

That’s common sense. Because “roving patrols” without clear rules don’t just create fear, they create mistakes. They create confrontations. And they create the kind of murky, confusing encounters where nobody knows who’s in charge, what authority is being used, or what rights people actually have in that moment.  

And that’s where the second point matters: a universal code of conduct and independent investigations. If local police departments can be required to follow use-of-force policies, federal agents shouldn’t get a special carve-out. Accountability isn’t anti-law enforcement — it’s what makes law enforcement legitimate.  

Then there’s the third demand, and honestly, this one shouldn’t even be controversial:  

“Third, we want masks off, body cameras on. Federal agents must be prohibited from wearing masks, they must be required to wear body cameras and they also must always have to carry proper identification.”  

Masks off. Body cameras on. Proper identification. That’s not about politics, it’s about basic transparency.  

Body cameras have been used across the country because they protect the public and they protect officers when accusations fly. And if someone is exercising government power — stopping you, detaining you, entering your home — you should be able to see their face and know who they are.  

The public is clearly uneasy with how far things are going right now. A Reuters/Ipsos poll found that some 58 percent of respondents said ICE agents have gone “too far” in their crackdown. That’s not a fringe opinion, that’s a majority.  

So when people say Democrats don’t have a plan, here it is: guardrails, accountability, and transparency. Because enforcing immigration law should never mean abandoning the standards that make this country a democracy in the first place. 

Lindsey Granger is a NewsNation contributor and co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising.” This column is an edited transcription of her on-air commentary.
